reasoning: Candidate A has the stronger first viewport: the sample alert is immediately legible, co-located with the free CTA, and resolves the core 'is this real?' doubt with less friction. However, Candidate B is stronger for the full brief because it actually operationalizes the three-segment audience model: family planner safety proof, superfan comparison/pro cadence depth, and an agency inquiry path. B also provides an inline desktop free-alert task path, while A stops at a CTA and pushes criteria entry downstream. Both candidates fail the programmatic gate via Section 12 count mismatch, but A has the additional missing-component coherence failure. A carries heavier generic landing-page trope risk through repeated equal-card rows, stats strip, testimonial cards, pricing cards, and bottom FAQ. B also has trope exposure, but its segment-specific tables, grouped coverage, and agency form make the structure more outcome-specific.
best_reusable_move_a: The hero sample alert mockup is the clearest proof artifact: specific restaurant, party size, date/time, Disney handoff CTA, and latency note all directly answer whether the service is real and useful.
best_reusable_move_b: The optional segment guide followed by dedicated family, superfan, and agency sections gives each audience a relevant path without forcing segmentation before the primary free-alert action.
